network.overall_strategy: This network models adenine as a multi-entry, multi-environment prebiotic target centered on HCN chemistry and the shared hub AICN. Hydrothermal settings provide reduced carbon feedstocks and formamide/HCN precursors through mineral-catalyzed CO2 reduction and formamide-generating chemistry. Surface environments concentrate HCN by evaporation or freezing analogs, support classical HCN oligomerization through DAMN and AICN, enable formamide condensation on mineral catalysts, and drive UV/cyanosulfidic photochemistry. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete adenine release from nucleoside-level precursors or allow nucleotide-linked adenine availability. The network emphasizes literature-validated convergence on HCN, DAMN, formamide, formamidine, and especially AICN, then branches into ten distinct literature-grounded routes to adenine itself or adenine-releasing adenine-containing precursors.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 over Fe-S phases generates formate as an early reduced C1 carrier.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis of CO2 reduction is consistent with vent chemistry and iron-sulfur world models; greigite/mackinawite are required hydrothermal minerals and provide a plausible source of reduced C1 compounds feeding later formamide chemistry (Wachtershauser, 1988-1992; Cody et al., 2000; LaRowe & Regnier, 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formamide generation from reduced C1 feedstocks

conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal concentration and thermophoretic retention in pores

reactions.mechanism: Reduced C1 species and ammonia are converted under hydrothermal dehydration/condensation conditions into formamide-enriched fluids.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide accumulation in hydrothermal pores has been argued to be feasible and relevant to nucleobase synthesis; this connecting step is partly inferential but used to link hydrothermal C1 reduction to the experimentally validated formamide reaction network (Niether et al., 2016; Saladino et al., 2001; Bada & Cleaves, 2015).

reactions.name: Formamide thermal decomposition to HCN

conditions.other: drying film or neat formamide, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Heating formamide generates HCN and NH3, which feed classical HCN oligomerization chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly identifies the equilibrium HCONH2 ⇌ HCN + H2O and thermal decomposition of formamide to HCN/NH3/HNCO as a mechanistic basis for nucleobase formation from formamide (Saladino et al., 2001; Barks et al., 2010; Slavova et al., 2020; Sponer et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Classical HCN dimerization to iminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: base-rich mineral surfaces with NH3 in solution
conditions.other: pH 8-9, concentrated NH4CN or evaporitic concentration

reactions.mechanism: CN- attacks HCN to form the HCN dimer iminoacetonitrile under basic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first mechanistic step in Oró-type adenine synthesis, with computational support for ammonia/base catalysis and experimental requirement for concentrated NH4CN at basic pH (Oro, 1960; Oro & Kimball, 1961, 1962; Kikuchi et al., 2000; Sandstrom & Rahm, 2023).

reactions.name: HCN trimerization to aminomalononitrile

conditions.other: pH 8-9, concentrated cyanide solutions or eutectic concentration

reactions.mechanism: Sequential addition of a third HCN to the dimer produces the reactive trimer aminomalononitrile.
reactions.reasoning: AMN is the accepted trimer intermediate in the Oró/Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel sequence and is supported by mechanistic and computational studies of HCN oligomerization (Sanchez et al., 1967; Roy et al., 2007; Sandstrom & Rahm, 2025/2026).

reactions.name: HCN tetramerization to DAMN

conditions.other: NH4CN-rich solutions, pH 8-9; DAMN isolable at appreciable fraction of HCN oligomer mixture

reactions.mechanism: Addition of the fourth HCN equivalent yields stable diaminomaleonitrile, the key photochemical precursor.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the first stable, isolable intermediate in adenine synthesis from HCN and a necessary node in classical and modern models (Zubay & Mui, 2001; Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Sandstrom & Rahm, 2025/2026).

reactions.name: UV photoisomerization of DAMN to trans-DAMN

conditions.other: UV irradiation 298-350 nm under young-Sun flux

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cis/trans photoisomerization prepares DAMN for further photochemical rearrangement toward imidazole-ring formation.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris and Orgel identified UV-driven DAMN photochemistry en route to AICN, and later work quantified rapid AICN generation under young-Sun UV; trans-DAMN is an established intermediate in that sequence (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Michaelian & Simeonov, 2022).

reactions.name: Photochemical rearrangement of trans-DAMN to AICN

conditions.other: young-Sun UV, three-photon sequence via ketenimine; seconds to minutes irradiation

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical rearrangement proceeds via ketenimine-like intermediates to produce 4-aminoimidazole-5-carbonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: AICN is the central prebiotic purine hub generated from DAMN by UV photochemistry; this step is directly established experimentally and further analyzed in photophysical models (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Sanchez et al., 1968; Michaelian & Simeonov, 2022).

reactions.name: AICN ring closure with HCN to adenine

conditions.catalyst: NH3-assisted aqueous medium on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: pH 8-9; kinetically bottlenecked final pentamerization step

reactions.mechanism: AICN captures a fifth HCN equivalent and cyclizes to the purine skeleton, yielding adenine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical final step of the Oró/Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel pathway; theory identifies it as the kinetic bottleneck, with NH3 catalysis greatly reducing the barrier (Sanchez et al., 1968; Roy et al., 2007; Armas-Vazquez et al., 2023).

reactions.name: AICN ring closure with formamidine to adenine

conditions.other: formamidine as one-carbon/one-nitrogen donor under evaporitic concentration

reactions.mechanism: Formamidine reacts with AICN to complete pyrimidine ring annulation and give adenine.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris and Orgel established AICN + HCN or formamidine as the branching entry to adenine; formamidine is a key literature-specified donor in the purine branch network (Sanchez et al., 1968; Zubay & Mui, 2001).

reactions.name: Ammonium formate-mediated conversion of DAMN to adenine

conditions.other: 110C dry heating; ammonium formate generates formamidine in situ

reactions.mechanism: DAMN undergoes thermal conversion with ammonium formate, bypassing the UV AICN route and producing adenine via in situ formamidine.
reactions.reasoning: Hill and Orgel reported ~18% adenine from DAMN plus ammonium formate at 110C, the highest yield from a plausibly prebiotic one-pot reaction; formamidine is generated in situ and the pathway bypasses the photochemical step (Hill & Orgel, 2002).

reactions.name: Mineral-catalyzed adenine synthesis directly from formamide
reactions.mechanism: Formamide thermally condenses on mineral surfaces through HCN-generating and imidazole-forming sub-networks that produce AICN and adenine in one-pot chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Multiple studies showed adenine formation from neat formamide at 100-160C on TiO2 and montmorillonite, with HCN-like intermediacy and AICN-consistent pathways; this reaction condenses that validated mineral-catalyzed network into a connected node (Saladino et al., 2001; Saladino et al., 2003; Saladino et al., 2004; Barks et al., 2010; Slavova et al., 2020).

reactions.name: UV-assisted formamide synthesis of adenine

conditions.catalyst: UV with or without mineral photocatalysis

reactions.mechanism: Irradiated formamide generates reactive CN-containing species and purine intermediates, including AICN, leading to adenine.
reactions.reasoning: Barks et al. demonstrated adenine production from UV-irradiated formamide at 130C even without minerals; TiO2 is included here as an allowed surface photocatalyst that is independently known to drive formamide photochemistry (Barks et al., 2010; Saladino et al., 2003).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic generation of thiocyanate donor
reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived cyano chemistry and sulfide under UV establish thiocyanate-family sulfur/nitrogen donors used in purine-thio pathways.
reactions.reasoning: This connector reaction represents the sulfurized cyano chemistry needed for the Xu et al. purine nucleoside route; it follows the cyanosulfidic systems framework based on HCN, H2S, UV, and metal/sulfide photoredox chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Formation of 8-mercaptoadenine from triaminopyrimidine and thiocyanate chemistry

conditions.catalyst: MgCl2-compatible neutral cyanosulfidic medium on mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Sulfurized purine assembly gives 8-mercaptoadenine as a photoreducible adenine precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Xu et al. identified 8-mercaptoadenine, derived from triaminopyrimidine plus ammonium thiocyanate, as the key precursor in selective prebiotic DNA purine nucleoside synthesis (Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreductive conversion of 8-mercaptoadenine to adenine and deoxyadenosine

conditions.catalyst: UV with H2S and MgCl2-enabled cyanosulfidic photochemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction removes sulfur from 8-mercaptoadenine-level intermediates, yielding adenine and adenine nucleosides under selective cyanosulfidic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Xu et al. showed that 8-mercaptoadenine-based intermediates undergo H2S/UV photoreduction to produce adenine-containing DNA and RNA purine nucleosides with high selectivity under cyanosulfidic conditions (Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Prebiotic hydrolysis of adenosine or deoxyadenosine to free adenine

conditions.other: geothermal cycling or mildly acidic hydrolytic pool conditions

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of the glycosidic bond releases adenine from adenine nucleosides in aqueous prebiotic settings.
reactions.reasoning: This connecting reaction is included to close routes in which adenine first appears as a nucleoside-level product in the cyanosulfidic literature; while the cited work focuses on nucleoside synthesis rather than hydrolysis, glycosidic bond cleavage is chemically standard and necessary here to connect known adenine-containing products back to the free-base target. The step is therefore explicitly connective and partly speculative but consistent with prebiotic hydrolysis chemistry (Xu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Becker et al., 2016; Becker et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Classical Oró HCN pentamerization
pathways.description: Canonical surface HCN oligomerization through dimer, trimer, DAMN, AICN, and final HCN capture to adenine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface HCN route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formate and formamide, which decompose to HCN and then enter the classical HCN route to adenine.

pathways.name: AICN plus formamidine closure
pathways.description: Classical HCN oligomerization to AICN followed by ring closure using formamidine rather than HCN.

pathways.name: Hill-Orgel ammonium formate bypass
pathways.description: DAMN formed from HCN is thermally converted to adenine with ammonium formate at 110C, bypassing the UV AICN photochemical branch.

pathways.name: Direct mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation on TiO2
pathways.description: Neat formamide in surface geothermal settings condenses directly to AICN and adenine on TiO2.

pathways.name: Direct mineral-catalyzed formamide condensation on montmorillonite
pathways.description: Parallel one-pot surface condensation of formamide to adenine on clay mineral surfaces.

pathways.name: UV-heated formamide route
pathways.description: Formamide irradiated at 130C yields adenine through CN-rich photochemistry with AICN-like intermediates.

pathways.name: Formamide-to-HCN-to-adenine route
pathways.description: Formamide first decomposes to HCN, then feeds the classical Oró route to adenine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic 8-mercaptoadenine free-base route
pathways.description: Sulfurized purine chemistry generates 8-mercaptoadenine, which is photoreduced under cyanosulfidic conditions to adenine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic nucleoside-first route with adenine release
pathways.description: The Xu cyanosulfidic system first yields deoxyadenosine/adenosine, which can hydrolytically release adenine in later prebiotic pools.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature alignment with the classical Oró/Ferris-Orgel route. The sequence HCN dimerization/trimerization/tetramerization to DAMN, UV isomerization to trans-DAMN, rearrangement to AICN, and final closure to adenine is the best-supported free-base adenine pathway in the literature. Main weakness is that the network summary does not state the critical concentration, pH 8-9, or UV wavelength constraints, and the final AICN + HCN closure is known to be kinetically difficult.
suggestions: Specify pH 8-9 and concentrated/eutectic HCN conditions for oligomerization
Note UV range near 298-350 nm for DAMN photochemistry
Explicitly acknowledge the kinetic bottleneck for AICN + HCN ring closure

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is topologically coherent and does capture hydrothermal-to-surface transfer, but its weakest point is the speculative hydrothermal generation of formamide from formate plus ammonia and the implied hydrothermal feed to HCN chemistry. Literature supports hydrothermal concentration of formamide and some vent relevance, but free adenine accumulation at high hydrothermal temperatures is problematic. Surface continuation through HCN oligomerization is much stronger than the hydrothermal front-end.
suggestions: Constrain hydrothermal conditions to mild alkaline vent or pore settings rather than generic high-temperature vent chemistry
Use thermophoretic concentration of formamide in pores as the hydrothermal link instead of implying efficient direct vent synthesis
Clarify environmental transfer from vent products to evaporitic or freezing surface reservoirs

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent alignment with literature identifying AICN as a hub and formamidine as an alternative ring-closing donor to adenine. This pathway is chemically more favorable than relying only on AICN + HCN. The summary would be stronger if the source of formamidine were explicitly justified from HCN oligomerization mixtures or ammonium formate decomposition.
suggestions: Explicitly connect formamidine availability to ammonium formate or HCN-derived mixtures
Add pH and concentration constraints for the AICN-formamidine step
State that this route helps bypass the worst kinetic limitations of direct AICN + HCN closure

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best-supported and highest-yielding prebiotically plausible adenine route in the supplied literature. DAMN plus ammonium formate at 110°C aligns directly with Hill & Orgel and correctly captures in situ formamidine generation. It is concise, chemically sound, and avoids dependence on the low-efficiency photochemical bottleneck.
suggestions: Specify the 110°C dry-heating or concentrated-solution regime explicitly
Indicate that ammonium formate acts as a formamidine precursor in situ
Highlight this as one of the most credible productive adenine nodes in the network

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The overall claim is literature-supported: TiO2-catalyzed heating of formamide can produce adenine and AICN-containing mixtures. However, as encoded, the single reaction is over-compressed because one node simultaneously gives AICN, adenine, and HCN from formamide without intermediate detail. That is acceptable as a network abstraction, but chemically it conflates multiple radical and condensation substeps.
suggestions: Represent AICN as the major intermediate and adenine as a downstream product rather than co-product in one lumped step
Specify TiO2 photocatalysis or heated neat formamide conditions separately
Include approximate 100-160°C conditions from the formamide literature

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Montmorillonite-catalyzed adenine formation from formamide is supported by Saladino's work, so the route is plausible. The issue is again granularity: the single-step representation suppresses mechanistic branching through HCN/AICN and mixed purine products. Still, as a network shortcut it is acceptable.
suggestions: Differentiate the TiO2 and montmorillonite variants as separate reaction nodes if both are intended
Show AICN explicitly as a dominant hub intermediate in the formamide branch
Specify neat formamide and heating conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: UV-assisted adenine formation from formamide is well aligned with Barks et al. and related formamide irradiation literature. As with the mineral-catalyzed one-step routes, this is an acceptable coarse-grained abstraction, though it would benefit from better separation of HCN generation, AICN accumulation, and adenine formation.
suggestions: Specify UV irradiation around 130°C or equivalent reported conditions
Clarify whether mineral-free or mineral-assisted irradiation is intended
Differentiate direct adenine production from AICN-mediated subnetwork chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong integrative route linking formamide chemistry to classical HCN pentamerization. This matches the literature view that formamide and HCN chemistry are connected by equilibrium and shared intermediates. The main concern is whether decomposition and concentration conditions are sufficiently specified to avoid excessive dilution and side-product formation.
suggestions: Specify neat or concentrated formamide rather than dilute aqueous formamide
Include mention of tar/asphalt side products reducing selectivity
Note that formamide acts as a practical feedstock partly because of its high boiling point and concentration potential

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The terminal steps align well with Xu et al. on 8-mercaptoadenine photoreduction to adenine under cyanosulfidic conditions. The main weakness is the precursor handling: the network uses thiocyanic acid as a donor but the literature route is better framed through ammonium thiocyanate/thiocyanate chemistry and triaminopyrimidine, with Mg2+, phosphate-buffered neutral conditions, UV, and H2S/Cu photoredox context. Missing provenance for triaminopyrimidine also weakens coherence.
suggestions: Specify ammonium thiocyanate or thiocyanate salt chemistry rather than only thiocyanic acid
Add the missing source of triaminopyrimidine within the network or state it as an imported precursor
Include Cu(I)/Cu(II), phosphate, MgCl2, and ~35°C neutral-pH cyanosulfidic conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The nucleoside-first concept reflects modern literature and is valuable, but the final hydrolysis step is weakly represented. Reaction 17 uses both deoxyadenosine and adenosine as inputs to produce adenine, which is not chemically tidy as a single reaction and does not match a specific prebiotic release pathway well. This branch is more a salvage/decomposition concept than a productive adenine synthesis route.
suggestions: Split adenosine and deoxyadenosine hydrolysis into separate reactions
Provide explicit hydrolysis/deglycosylation conditions and plausibility justification
Treat nucleoside formation as an alternative target branch rather than a core route to free adenine unless release chemistry is well supported

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong, literature-aware adenine synthesis network with excellent redundancy, clear hub architecture, and real convergence on AICN, HCN, DAMN, formamide, and formamidine. It succeeds especially well in reflecting the modern view that adenine synthesis is a network rather than a single linear route. Topologically, it is well connected and includes multiple convergent routes to the target. Chemically, the classical HCN route, AICN/formamidine closure, Hill-Orgel ammonium formate bypass, and formamide-based pathways are all well aligned with the supplied literature. The cyanosulfidic branch is directionally correct and captures high-value recent work, but it is less complete because triaminopyrimidine is not sourced and some conditions are underspecified. The largest deficiencies are formal rather than conceptual: the reaction list as presented omits required mineral-agent specification for hydrothermal and surface reactions, some one-step formamide nodes are over-compressed, and the hydrothermal front-end is the least well supported because vent conditions are generally poor for adenine stability and direct formamide synthesis is not as firmly established as the surface chemistry. Reaction 17 is the weakest step in the whole network. Literature alignment is otherwise very good: the network properly centers AICN, includes the Hill & Orgel high-yield route, recognizes formamide as an alternative feedstock, and includes cyanosulfidic nucleoside-first chemistry. Because the JSON summary lacks explicit per-reaction citations and mineral agents, and because a few steps are too lumped or insufficiently sourced, the network falls short of approval at the strict >=0.9 threshold despite being scientifically strong overall.
